report_title: US Aviation Parts International Trade Data Analysis
data_sources: US Census Bureau Foreign Trade Division

section_1_global_aircraft_parts_trade_HS.description: HS 8803 - Parts of aircraft, spacecraft, balloons, dirigibles, gliders

2019.note: Pre-COVID peak. Aircraft parts were the world's 27th most traded product.

2020.note: COVID crash. Passenger traffic down 96% in April 2020. Orders for commercial aircraft dropped ~55%.

2021.note: Partial recovery. US aircraft parts exports were $9.4B in Q1 2021 vs $15.2B in Q1 2019 (still 38% below).

2022.note: Strong recovery year. Fastest growing importers: Canada ($864M growth), Germany ($846M), UK ($645M), US ($633M).

2023.note: Full recovery and growth beyond 2019 levels.

2024.note: Global aircraft parts trade essentially flat vs 2023, indicating market maturation.

top_global_exporters_2024_worldstopexpor.top_5_share: 57.3% of global aircraft parts export revenues

section_2_US_trade_position.us_broader_aerospace_exports_2024: ~$123B (aircraft parts are the nation's top export product)
section_2_US_trade_position.note: CRITICAL DISTINCTION: The US is a slight net IMPORTER of aircraft parts (HS 8803) but a massive net EXPORTER in broader aerospace (which includes complete aircraft). The $33B net import figure cited in Section 232 covers commercial aircraft + engines + parts combined.

section_3_US_imports_by_country.description: Countries the US IMPORTS aircraft parts FROM (where parts are sourced cheaply)

section_232_import_sources_combined_airc.total_net_import_deficit: $33B more imports than exports (commercial aircraft + engines + parts)

section_3_US_imports_by_country.arbitrage_signal: Parts flow INTO the US primarily from UK, Japan, France, Mexico, and Canada. These are countries where parts can be sourced at lower effective cost.

section_4_US_exports_by_country.description: Countries the US EXPORTS aircraft parts TO (where demand/prices are higher)
section_4_US_exports_by_country.arbitrage_signal: Parts flow OUT of the US primarily to Japan, Italy, South Korea, Israel, Australia - these represent markets where US-sourced parts command premium prices or where local supply is limited.

price_by_origin.arbitrage_ratio: 12.6x price differential between cheapest and most expensive origin

HS_8803_10_propellers_and_rotors.trend: 6.5% import volume growth in 2023, fourth consecutive year of growth

HS_8803_20_undercarriages_landing_gear.trade_ratio: US imports 7.5x more landing gear than it exports
HS_8803_20_undercarriages_landing_gear.arbitrage_signal: Massive import dependency for landing gear - the US imports nearly 8x what it exports monthly. Safran (France) dominates supply.

HS_8803_30_other_parts_airplanes_helicop.note: By far the largest subcategory, representing ~79% of all HS 8803 trade

section_6_turbojet_engine_parts_HS8411_9.description: HS 8411.91 - Parts of turbojet or turbopropeller engines (ENGINE parts specifically)
section_6_turbojet_engine_parts_HS8411_9.arbitrage_signal: France dominates engine parts supply to the US ($4.8B). Singapore is a surprise #4 globally in engine parts exports ($7.52B) - it has become a major engine MRO hub. UK engine parts exports ($12.92B) are massive - Rolls-Royce effect.
section_6_turbojet_engine_parts_HS8411_9.key_insight: Engine parts (HS 8411.91) represent a larger trade flow than aircraft structural parts (HS 8803). The US imported $15.53B in engine parts in 2019 alone.

section_7_top_importing_companies.description: Largest US importers of aircraft parts (HTS 8803) from customs data
top_importers_HTS_8803: Airbus Americas (AL) - significant importer for Mobile assembly line
Mitsubishi Logistics America Corporation (WA) - logistics for Japanese aerospace
section_7_top_importing_companies.engine_market_concentration: 4 companies (Rolls-Royce, Pratt & Whitney, CFM International, GE) control 99% of global aero engine market
section_7_top_importing_companies.raw_material_imports: 16% of major US aircraft manufacturers import raw materials, significantly higher than manufacturing sector average
section_7_top_importing_companies.note: Individual shipment-level customs data is available through commercial providers (ImportKey, Datamyne, ImportInfo) but requires paid subscriptions. Free public data only shows aggregate trade flows.

2020_COVID_crash.passenger_traffic_drop: 96% below 2019 in April 2020, 60% below for full year

2021_partial_recovery.parts_sourcing_behavior: Airlines cannibalized aircraft parts and green-time engines to avoid maintenance costs, starving aftermarket supply chain
2021_partial_recovery.purchasing_reduction: Significant reduction in new parts purchasing in 2020-2021

2022_strong_recovery.supply_chain_fragility: Still unable to receive parts when needed, extending beyond 2023
2022_strong_recovery.worker_shortages: Key constraint alongside stressed global transportation
2022_strong_recovery.fastest_growing_importers: Canada, Germany, UK, US, Spain all surged

2023_full_recovery.MRO_labor_costs: +7.3% worldwide, with 11.5% attrition in North America

trade_flow_changes.shift_1: COVID created a parts SHORTAGE as airlines stopped buying and cannibalized existing fleet
trade_flow_changes.shift_2: Post-COVID, parts demand surged but supply chain couldn't keep up, creating price inflation
trade_flow_changes.shift_3: Nearshoring trend: ~33% of operators and 50% of OEMs/MROs expect to nearshore more, especially airframe and components
trade_flow_changes.shift_4: Asia-Pacific emergence: India and Southeast Asia gaining share due to competitive labor costs

Mexico.advantage: Low labor cost + USMCA trade agreement (no tariffs)

South_Africa.advantage: Propeller/rotor parts at $63,814/ton vs UK at $807,447/ton (12.6x cheaper)

India.advantage: Competitive labor costs, central geographic location, growing MRO capacity
India.growth_potential: High - government actively promoting aerospace manufacturing

Singapore.advantage: 10% of global MRO market, $8B aerospace industry, deep expertise
Singapore.role: Major engine overhaul and parts hub for Asia-Pacific

Japan.role: Precision manufacturing, key Boeing supply chain partner

Israel.why_premium: Military/defense demand, limited local supply, geopolitical urgency

South_Korea.why_premium: Growing airline fleet, limited domestic aerospace manufacturing

Australia.why_premium: Geographic isolation, small domestic industry, defense requirements

Brazil.why_premium: Large domestic aviation market, Embraer supply chain, import tariffs

Middle_East.note: Saudi Arabia received $1.97B in US aerospace exports in 2021
Middle_East.why_premium: Massive fleet expansion (Emirates, Qatar, Etihad, Saudia), limited local MRO

France.explanation: France sends parts (Safran, Airbus supply chain) while buying complete systems and US-origin parts
France.net_flow: US is a massive NET EXPORTER to France in broader aerospace

Germany.explanation: German industry (Airbus Hamburg, MTU, Liebherr) both produces and consumes massive volumes

Canada.explanation: Deeply integrated supply chain (Bombardier, Pratt & Whitney Canada, CAE)

section_10_section_232_tariff_risk.scope: Commercial aircraft, jet engines, and parts for commercial aircraft and jet engines
section_10_section_232_tariff_risk.potential_tariff: 25% (based on pattern from steel/aluminum Section 232 tariffs)
section_10_section_232_tariff_risk.import_exposure: $33B net import deficit in commercial aircraft + engines + parts
section_10_section_232_tariff_risk.industry_opposition: US Chamber of Commerce, Business Roundtable, 205 stakeholders submitted comments opposing tariffs. Imported regional planes contain 40-50% US content.
section_10_section_232_tariff_risk.arbitrage_implication: If 25% tariffs are imposed on EU/Canada aircraft parts, this would DRAMATICALLY change trade flows and create massive arbitrage opportunities for parts sourced from non-tariffed countries (Mexico under USMCA, Singapore, India, Japan). Parts stockpiling before tariff implementation would be a major trade.

labor_cost_by_region.North_America: Highest cost, 11.5% attrition rate, 7.3% labor cost increase in 2023
labor_cost_by_region.Europe: High cost but stable, strong engineering workforce
labor_cost_by_region.Asia_Pacific: Lowest cost of skilled labor vs North America and Europe
labor_cost_by_region.India: Competitive labor costs, central geographic location

section_11_MRO_regional_cost_differentia.nearshoring_trend: 1 in 3 operators and 50% of OEMs/MROs plan to nearshore more, especially airframe and components

section_12_key_arbitrage_conclusions.finding_1: The US is the world's largest BOTH exporter ($12.2B) and importer ($13.3B) of aircraft parts, with a small net import deficit of $1.14B. This means massive cross-border flows in BOTH directions = arbitrage opportunities.
section_12_key_arbitrage_conclusions.finding_2: Propeller/rotor parts show a 12.6x price differential between cheapest origin (South Africa $63,814/ton) and most expensive (UK $807,447/ton). This is the single largest documented price arbitrage in aviation parts.
section_12_key_arbitrage_conclusions.finding_3: Landing gear (HS 8803.20) shows extreme US import dependency - importing 7.5x more than exporting. Safran (France) dominates this market. Any supply disruption = price spike opportunity.
section_12_key_arbitrage_conclusions.finding_4: Engine parts (HS 8411.91) are a BIGGER market than structural parts. France alone supplies $4.8B to the US. Singapore has emerged as the #4 global engine parts exporter at $7.52B.
section_12_key_arbitrage_conclusions.finding_5: COVID created a massive dislocation: 34.3% drop in global parts trade, followed by supply chain shortages, cannibalization of existing fleet, and 8.3% material cost inflation. Post-COVID arbitrage persists as supply hasn't fully caught up with demand.
section_12_key_arbitrage_conclusions.finding_6: Section 232 tariffs (potentially 25%) on EU and Canadian aircraft parts would be the single largest trade policy disruption. ~75% of US aircraft parts imports come from EU + Canada. This creates pre-tariff stockpiling opportunities and post-tariff sourcing shifts to Mexico, Singapore, India, Japan.
section_12_key_arbitrage_conclusions.finding_7: Asia-Pacific MRO is growing fastest. Parts flowing from low-cost Asian sources (Singapore, India, Japan) to high-cost Western markets represent the primary geographic arbitrage vector going forward.
section_12_key_arbitrage_conclusions.finding_8: 4 companies control 99% of the aero engine market. This concentration means parts pricing is NOT truly competitive - OEM monopoly pricing creates persistent arbitrage between OEM-sourced and aftermarket/PMA parts.
